feed.title: Arjun Krishna Blog
feed.subtitle: In this blog I will explain my projects put up on github, report some data analysis I perform and also put up notes on some interesting topics.

entry.title: Multi Layer Perceptron
entry.content:
CONTENTS

  • Description of the Network
  • Forward-Propagation
  • Back-Propagation
    • Gradient Descent Update
    • Back-Propagation Algorithm
      • Mini-Batch Back-Propagation ( with momentum based acceleration )
  • MNIST Classification
    • Data
    • Preprocessing
    • Architecture
    • Training
    • Experiment
      • Sigmoid Activation
      • ReLU Activation
      • Sigmoid Activation (Data not standardized)
      • Performance on a Test Sample
    • Conclusion
  • Link to the code

Description of the Network

Symbol Dimension Description
$n_l$ 1 Number of layers
$x$ ($n$, $1$) Input Image Flattened
$a^{(l)}$ ($d_{(l)}$, $1$) Activation in Hidden Layer $(l)$
$\hat{y}$ ($c$, $1$) Prediction of the Neural Net
$W^{(l)}$ ($d_{(l)}$,$d_{(l-1)}$) Weight of the connections layer $(l-1)$ to $(l)$
$W_{ji}^{(l)}$ 1 Weight of edge connecting $j^{th}$ neuron in layer $(l)$ to $i^{th}$ neuron in layer $(l-1)$
$b^{(l)}$ ($d_{(l)}$, $1$) Bias for neurons in layer $(l)$
$f$ - Activation Function


Also, Note that

$a^{(1)} = x$

$\hat{y} = a^{(n_l)}$

Forward-Propagation

Back-Propagation

For the Classification Task we choose cross entropy as the loss function we want to minimize. The following is the cross entropy loss for a training example.

  • Let m is the number of training samples
  • Also applying an $L_2$ regularization on the weights in the network The following the Loss function we would want to minimize.

Gradient Descent Update

Back-Propagation Algorithm

  • Perform the forward pass

  • For the error at the output layer

    The matrix equivalent of the same (y is a one-hot encoded representation of the label)

  • For l = $n_l - 1$ to $2$ :

    Set

  • Compute the gradients


Mini-Batch Back-Propagation ( with momentum based acceleration )

  • For a mini-batch of size of m, we compute the average gradient form the batch and use that to update the model parameters.

  • Parameter Update using Momentum based acceleration

    Initially, $vW^{(l)}$ and $vb^{(l)}$ are zero matrices of the same size as $W^{(l)}$ and $b^{(l)}$ respectively.

MNIST Classification

Data

The input is an $28 \times 28$ image of a handwritten digit, the task is to classify the image to recognize the digit. The MNIST data from Lecun’s page is used for this task. The training data contains $60000$ images and the test data has $10000$ images.

Preprocessing

The flattened image to be feed into the network is standardized (normalized). Each pixel is a feature and each feature is standardized by centralizing (subtract the mean) and divided by the standard deviation. This was done because when using ReLU activations in the MLP, the softmax exploded into a NaN loss and hindered further training. However, the MLP which used sigmoid activations could be trained by directly feeding the image without the preprocessing.

Architecture

A Multilayer Perceptron with 3 hidden layers. The input is a $784$ dimensional vector $x$, followed by $h_1$(1000), $h_2$(500), $h_3$(250) and the output $y$ is a 10 dimensional vector, representing the probability of image belonging to a particular class label. The parameters of the network are initialized with a random gaussian with mean 0 and standard deviation 0.08.

Training

The SGD algorithm with momentum based acceleration is used for training. The size of the minibatch is 64. The L2-regularization parameter $\lambda = 0.005$ and the momentum parameter $\gamma = 0.8$.

Experiment

In this section, the plots of the training progress of the Multilayer perceptron using Sigmoid and ReLU activations are presented for different learning rates (fixed and scheduled).

Sigmoid Activation

The learning rates chosen are 0.001, 0.01, 0.05 and a scheduled learning rate with initial value 0.2 and decayed by a factor of 0.85 every 250 iterations. The input to the network is standardized. Achieves a test-accuracy of $90.1$%

The lower the learning rate, the slower is the convergence. A very high learning rate, though rushes to the optimum at a rapid pace and has a lot of fluctuations in the test loss. The learning rate scheduling works best, initially moves rapidly (taking larger steps) and then lowers the learning rate to steadily move to an optimum.

Training Loss vs Epoch for different Learning rates


The training loss plots also indicate the rate of convergence is dependent on the learning rate. The lower learning rate the convergence is slower. The scheduled learning rate, has lesser fluctuation in training loss as the training progresses and hence more stable.

ReLU Activation

The learning rates chosen are 0.001, 0.01, 0.05 and a scheduled learning rate with initial value 0.2 and decayed by a factor of 0.85 every 250 iterations. The input to the network is standardized. Achieves a test-accuracy of $96.8$%

The performance of ReLU activated network is better than that of sigmoid, it achieves a higher accuracy and also has a lower test loss. However, observe that the sigmoid activated MLP approximately reaches close to the least loss it achieves in about $1000$ epochs whereas the ReLU activated MLP takes approximately $2000$ epochs.

Training Loss vs Epoch for different Learning rates


The choice of the Learning rate affects the rate at which the network converges and also affects the stability. With $\alpha = 0.001$ the improvement is smooth, but takes a long time to converge, whereas $\alpha = 0.05$ reaches a low loss, but oscillates a lot. MLP with a scheduled learning rate converges with lesser oscillations and also achieves a good accuracy. Also compared to sigmoid activated MLP, the ReLU-MLP has lower test loss.

Sigmoid Activation (Data not standardized)

Here the input to the MLP is not standardized and the raw flattened image is feed to the network. The learning rates for which the experiment is conducted is 0.001, 0.01, 0.05 and a scheduled learning rate with initial value 0.2 and decayed by factor of 0.85 every 250 iterations. This model achieves a test-accuracy of $93.9$%

We observe that in this case a very high $\alpha$ can lead to divergence, since the image signal varies from 0 to 255, large changes in weight can lead to large variations in activation, hence in the case of $\alpha=0.05$ we observe that the loss is increasing. Thus, in most cases it is a good idea to standardize (normalize) the data.

Performance on a Test Sample

The top three predictions for the sampled test image made by the Sigmoid Activated MLP and ReLU Activated MLP are presented. Both the networks are trained with scheduled learning rate. The value in the table is “predicted_label (probability)”

Image Sigmoid ReLU
0 (0.975) 0 (0.997)
1 (0.913) 1 (0.993)
2 (0.905) 2 (0.991)
3 (0.417) 3 (0.724)
4 (0.813) 4 (0.983)
6 (0.571) 6 (0.629)
6 (0.600) 6 (0.976)
7 (0.970) 7 (0.999)
8 (0.796) 8 (0.946)
9 (0.543) 9 (0.631)


Conclusion

  • The choice of Learning rate in MLP plays an important role in convergence.

  • Learning rate scheduling helps in achieving a stable convergence, without fluctuating the parameter values much at the later stages of the training, it reaches the optimum steadily.

  • ReLU activated MLP seems to perform better than the Sigmoid activated MLP. However, a MLP with layers having ReLU and Sigmoid activation might give better results. (Will experiment with this soon)

Link to the code

Checkout the code @ MLP-code
entry.summary: Multi Layer Perceptron (implemented in python). Experimented on the MNIST data

entry.title: Computation Models of Cognition
entry.content:
Note : This post contains my notes from the CMC course at IITM 
       and also some views I have on the topic.

The idea of this post is discuss cognitive science & challenges in creating a human-level intelligence, study the current day tools/techniques we have and also discuss possible non-traditional methods.

CONTENTS

  • Preliminary Thoughts
    • Is brain the only place where the cognition happens?

Preliminary Thoughts

Is brain the only place where the cognition happens?

It is very true that our brain plays a very important role in cognition. However, there is the theory of Embodied cognition which indicates that the entire body of an organism shapes the cognitive abilities.

Say we had a very powerful computing machine and could simulate the neuron connections in the human brain, Can we generate natural-intelligence ? There is a gap in our understanding of how the brain works, but if we truely understand all the underlying physiology of human brain and emulate it on a machine is that sufficient for intelligence is a hard question to answer. Philosophically speaking it is possible there some meta-physical element is attached to human intelligence which we haven’t discovered. However, it is important to see what we can engineer than debate about philosophical aspects.

Also, there is question of Build vs Grow in Aritifical Intelligence. Human intelligence is shaped by experiences and memory, this enhances over time this is the “Growth of Intelligence”. However, many present day models for the task of language translation is a “Built Intelligence” we show it a lot of adult conversations/language during training, So the model has not learnt say the translation ability like how a human does.

“If our brains were simple enough for us to understand them, we’d be so simple that we couldn’t.”
– Ian Stewart, The Collapse of Chaos: Discovering Simplicity in a Complex World
entry.summary: Can we really model Human level intelligence? Can machines think like Humans ? What are the current tools we have that tries to model some sort of intelligence? Are they sufficient? Modeling human mind is a very challenging problem. The key challenge in this area is that the Human mind itself is used in studying the 'Mind'. The area has a major philosophical component, it is important to ponder on these but it is also very interesting to see what we can engineer. With the human-machine interface becoming better everyday, there are still miles to go for a true Artificial Human-Like Intelligence to be created.

entry.title: Multi Arm Bandit
entry.content:
Bandit Algorithms

Let, $\mathbb{A}$ be the set of arms of the bandit. Let $ K = | \mathbb{A} | $

, indicates the expected reward on pulling arm $a$ and $q(a)$ indicates the algorithm’s estimate for $q^*(a)$

Let $n(a)$ indicate the number of times arm $a$ was pulled in a run.

Experiment Setup

The three algorithms described are run for 1000 steps on a 10-arm bandit, this is done on 2000 different bandit problem instances and the results presented are the average of the performance of the algorithm on these instances.

The later section shows the results for a 1000-arm bandit run for 100000 steps, but averaged over 200 instances of the problem so will have larger variations in the graph.

10-ARM BANDIT

As we increase the parameter eps the exploration increases, thus we see that the algorithm tends to pick arms that yield better rewards. So naturally eps = 0.1, seems to have the best result. But if we keep increasing eps, we loose out on exploitation and hence the algorithm would have a very high regret.

The temperature parameter controls the amount of exploration, higher the temperature more the exploration. So, in the graph T = 0.01 is close to being a greedy algorithm ( always exploiting ) and T = 100 is close to a random algorithm ( more of exploring ). So when we achieve a balance between these extremes we achieve better results, like at T = 0.2 we get the best results form the softmax algorithm.

As we see from the experiment’s result, the UCB1 algorithm performs the best. The UCB1 algorithm picks the arm that can potentially yield better rewards, by picking the arm that has the maximum upper confidence bound, which includes the uncertainty in the current estimate of $q^*$, whereas the other two algorithms do not do this and pick the arm that has the best current estimate or explore a lot more. Thus, UCB1 has a lower regret than the other two algorithms.

1000-ARM BANDIT

The averaging was done for only 200 instances of different bandit problems as it was computationally time consuming. So the graphs presented have a higher variance than the 10-arm bandit case, but the trends remains the same, the greedy case ( eps = 0 ) the algorithm stops after picking a sub-optimal arm, but when eps = 0.1 it performs a good amount of exploration and achieves a lower regret.

At T = 10, the softmax learning algorithm performs a lot more exploration hence has a very high regret and the T = 0.01 case is similar to the greedy algorithm in performance, at T = 0.2 the algorithm achieves better results. Where T is the temperature parameter of the softmax algorithm.

As we see from the experiment’s result, the UCB1 algorithm performs the best.

entry.title: My R toolset
entry.content:
In this post I will share a few standard R snippets that would be useful for some Machine Learning Problems. I learnt these from the ISLR by Gareth James, Daniela Witten, Trevor Hastie and Robert Tibshirani, most examples are taken directly from this book.

CONTENTS

  • Some basics
  • Linear Regression
  • Train-Test Split
  • Logistic Regression
  • Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)
  • K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)
  • Cross Validation
  • Bootstrap
  • Decision Trees
  • Random Forest
  • Gradient Boosted Trees

Some basics

Linear Regression

Train-Test Split

Logistic Regression

Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA)

K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)

Cross Validation

Bootstrap

Decision Trees

Random Forest

Gradient Boosted Trees
entry.summary: some basic R commands that would be useful
